name: Forge Audit-Propagate Cycle
summary: A meta-agent system that scans, audits, and upgrades other projects' agent systems against a shared pattern library.
description: Four specialized roles cycle through audit-and-upgrade passes across a portfolio of projects. The forgemaster coordinates, the assayer scans for gaps, the smith applies upgrades, and the keeper maintains the pattern library. Each cycle produces a gap analysis that prioritizes work across all managed projects. Patterns flow upward from real usage into the shared library, then back down to other projects — a self-improving loop.
realWorldExample: thisminute-forge manages 8 projects (thisminute, mainmenu, rhizome, ops, sts2, balatro, forge.thisminute.org, agent-forge). Over 13 audit cycles, it cleaned 900+ line forums, retired dead patterns (messages/), added librarian roles, bootstrapped steward agents, and propagated improvements back to the template library.
whenToUse: When managing multiple projects that share agent system patterns and you want consistency, gap detection, and cross-pollination of improvements without manual coordination.
strengths: Self-improving — patterns extracted from real usage feed back into the template
Cross-project visibility — catches drift and regression across an entire portfolio
Separation of concerns — scanning, upgrading, and maintaining are distinct roles
Prioritization built in — audit produces ranked work items, not just a list
weaknesses: Overhead for small portfolios — not worth it for fewer than 3 projects
Audit fatigue — repeated stable scans add little value (mitigated by idle detection)
Smith changes can conflict with active development in target projects
Pattern library needs curation or it accumulates unused patterns

agents.name: Forgemaster
agents.description: Coordinates the audit-and-propagate cycle. Decides what to scan, what to upgrade, and in what order. Routes actionable feedback to the keeper.

agents.name: Assayer
agents.description: Scans all registered projects against the pattern library. Produces a gap analysis with maturity levels, adoption matrix, and prioritized upgrade plans.

agents.name: Smith
agents.description: Applies upgrades to one project at a time — creates role files, cleans forums, adds keeper roles, retires dead patterns. Works in the target project's codebase.

agents.name: Keeper
agents.description: Maintains the pattern library and project registry. Updates patterns based on what changed, extracts new patterns from real usage, keeps the registry accurate.

shared: Audit file (audits/current.md) — cumulative gap analysis with cycle notes
Pattern files (patterns/*.md) — reusable agent system patterns
Project registry (agents.md) — agent counts, maturity levels, role lists
memoryArchitecture.description: All state is shared. The audit file accumulates cycle notes so the forgemaster can see trends. Pattern files are the knowledge base that the assayer scans against.

diagram:
graph TD
  FM[Forgemaster] -->|spawn| AS[Assayer]
  AS -->|gap analysis| FM
  FM -->|spawn with tasks| SM[Smith]
  SM -->|results| FM
  FM -->|spawn with changes| KP[Keeper]
  KP -->|updated patterns| FM
  FM -->|next cycle| AS
  SM -.->|modifies| P1[Project 1]
  SM -.->|modifies| P2[Project 2]
  SM -.->|modifies| P3[Project N]
  KP -.->|maintains| PL[Pattern Library]

name: Checkpoint Cycle Development
summary: An orchestrator coordinates rapid build-test-fix iterations through disposable cycle agents, using a checkpoint file as the sole source of truth.
description: The orchestrator never reads source code directly — it reads a checkpoint file, spawns a cycle agent with a targeted task, receives a short summary back, and updates the checkpoint. Cycle agents are disposable workers that do one fix per spawn. Specialist agents (mod-builder, bot-builder, etc.) handle domain-specific work. The checkpoint accumulates what works, what doesn't, and what to never do, creating institutional memory without forum overhead.
realWorldExample: Replit Agent evolved from a simple ReAct loop to a multi-agent architecture with manager, editor, and verifier roles running plan-execute-verify cycles. sts2 (Slay the Spire 2 autopilot mod) ran 37 checkpoint cycles over multiple sessions, tracking features, blockers, and 'never do' rules.
whenToUse: Rapid iteration projects where you're grinding through a bug list or building incrementally. Best when the codebase has a single hot path and work is sequential rather than parallel.
strengths: Context protection — orchestrator stays lightweight, never bloated by source code
Institutional memory — checkpoint accumulates hard-won lessons (what works, what to never do)
Low overhead — no forum, no protocol, just a file and a loop
Disposable agents — each cycle starts clean, no stale context accumulation
Clear progress tracking — cycle number and status visible at a glance
weaknesses: Sequential bottleneck — one cycle at a time, orchestrator is the chokepoint
Checkpoint can grow stale if not pruned
Not suited for parallel workstreams across different domains
Cycle agents lack cross-cycle context — can re-attempt failed approaches if checkpoint is unclear

agents.name: Orchestrator
agents.description: Reads checkpoint, decides what to work on next, spawns the right specialist or cycle agent, processes results, updates checkpoint. Never reads source code directly.

agents.name: Cycle Agent
agents.description: Disposable worker. Reads checkpoint, makes one focused fix, builds, tests, updates checkpoint, returns ≤20 line summary. One fix per spawn.

agents.name: Domain Specialist
agents.description: Handles domain-specific work (e.g., mod code, bot logic, protocol layer). Spawned when the task requires deep domain knowledge rather than a quick fix.

shared: Checkpoint file (.claude/iteration_checkpoint.md) — cycle number, status, current blocker, what works, what doesn't, what to never do
Section index (.claude/*-index.md) — line ranges for navigating large files without reading them whole
memoryArchitecture.description: The checkpoint IS the coordination mechanism. No forum, no protocol. All agents read it first and update it last. Section indices prevent context bloat from large files.

diagram:
graph TD
  O[Orchestrator] -->|read| CP[Checkpoint]
  O -->|spawn + task| C[Cycle Agent]
  C -->|read| CP
  C -->|fix + test| CODE[Codebase]
  C -->|update| CP
  C -->|≤20 line summary| O
  O -->|update| CP
  O -->|next cycle| C

name: Protocol-Forum Product Team
summary: A multi-agent product team coordinated by shared protocol and forum, with a librarian preventing documentation decay.
description: All agents follow a shared startup protocol (read role, check timestamp, read architecture, scan forum, do work, report, update memory, reflect). The forum is the sole inter-agent communication channel — agents post findings, vote on proposals, and build on each other's work. A librarian agent prevents the forum from ballooning by archiving resolved threads. Without a librarian, forums grow from 50 to 900+ lines in a single session.
realWorldExample: thisminute (10 agents) and mainmenu (7 agents) both use this pattern. thisminute's forum grew from 51 to 621 lines in one active session before the librarian cleaned it. mainmenu hit 904 lines before the forge added a librarian role. After librarian adoption, both stabilized under 200 lines. An inter-agent messages/ directory was tried and retired — the forum was sufficient.
whenToUse: Multi-domain product development with 4+ agents where work is ongoing and agents need to build on each other's findings. The protocol ensures consistency, the forum enables asynchronous collaboration, and the librarian prevents entropy.
strengths: Asynchronous collaboration — agents don't need to be spawned simultaneously
Self-documenting — forum creates an audit trail of decisions and findings
Consistent startup — protocol eliminates per-agent onboarding drift
Scales to 10+ agents across multiple domains
Librarian prevents the main scaling failure mode (documentation bloat)
weaknesses: Forum overhead — agents spend context reading and writing forum posts
Requires librarian discipline — without cleanup, forums balloon and degrade context quality
Protocol rigidity — all agents follow the same steps even when some aren't relevant
Voting minimums can bottleneck decisions when few agents are active

agents.name: Orchestrator
agents.description: Coordinates work across all agents. Reads forum for recent activity, decides which specialist to spawn, provides context. Collects shutdown reflections.

agents.name: Builder
agents.description: Implements features and fixes bugs. Posts findings and blockers to the forum.

agents.name: Designer
agents.description: Handles UI/UX decisions, visual consistency, and frontend implementation.

agents.name: Skeptic
agents.description: Reviews code and strategy for correctness, edge cases, and robustness. Posts critiques to forum with evidence.

agents.name: Strategist
agents.description: Sets priorities based on project goals and skeptic findings. Posts recommendations to forum.

agents.name: Librarian
agents.description: Maintains documentation health. Archives resolved forum threads, updates memory files, keeps AGENTS.md and ref docs current. Critical for preventing documentation decay.

shared: FORUM.md — threaded inter-agent communication with voting
PROTOCOL.md — shared startup sequence for all agents
AGENTS.md + ref/*.md — architecture documentation, split by domain when large
private: memory/{agent}.md — per-agent persistent memory for cross-session learnings
memoryArchitecture.description: Forum is shared and append-only (until librarian archives). Memory is per-agent and persistent. Protocol is shared and stable. Architecture docs are shared and maintained by the librarian.

diagram:
graph TD
  O[Orchestrator] -->|spawn| B[Builder]
  O -->|spawn| D[Designer]
  O -->|spawn| SK[Skeptic]
  O -->|spawn| ST[Strategist]
  O -->|spawn| L[Librarian]
  B <-->|post/read| F[Forum]
  D <-->|post/read| F
  SK <-->|post/read| F
  ST <-->|post/read| F
  L -->|archive/clean| F
  L -->|maintain| DOCS[Docs & Memory]
  P[Protocol] -.->|startup sequence| B
  P -.->|startup sequence| D
  P -.->|startup sequence| SK
  P -.->|startup sequence| ST

name: Game Autopilot Stack
summary: Three-process architecture (game mod, external bot, streaming overlay) with 9 specialized agents for building an LLM-powered game-playing system.
description: A game mod reads state and executes actions inside the game process. An external bot connects via IPC protocol, makes decisions using LLM + hardcoded heuristics, and learns from runs. A streaming overlay shows reasoning to a Twitch audience. Nine agents cover the full development surface: orchestrator coordinates, domain specialists build each component, an analyst handles prompt engineering, an operator runs self-play sessions, and a skeptic reviews cross-component consistency.
realWorldExample: sts2 (Slay the Spire 2) uses a C#/Godot mod communicating via named pipes with a .NET bot, overlaid on OBS. The mod handles 40-60% of decisions via hardcoded fast-path (no LLM), the bot uses Claude for complex decisions with prompt caching (90% cost reduction), and a learning system evolves strategy across runs with confidence-tiered notes. Balatro adapts the same architecture with Lua/Steamodded mod and TCP transport.
whenToUse: Building an autonomous game-playing AI that needs to mod a closed-source game, make intelligent decisions, learn from experience, and optionally stream the process. The three-process split is key — the mod can't be too complex (crashes the game), and the bot needs clean separation for LLM integration.
strengths: Clean separation — game crashes don't kill the bot, bot failures don't crash the game
Hardcoded fast-path — trivial decisions skip LLM entirely (cost + speed)
Learning system — strategy improves across runs without human intervention
Streaming-native — overlay process is decoupled and optional
Protocol-based — mod and bot can be developed and tested independently
weaknesses: Three-process coordination complexity — launch sequence, health monitoring, reconnection all need handling
Game modding is brittle — game updates break reflection/hooks
LLM latency in real-time games — hardcoded fast-path is a necessity, not an optimization
Large development surface — 9 agents across 3 codebases in potentially different languages

agents.name: Orchestrator
agents.description: Coordinates all development. Reads checkpoint, spawns specialists, tracks progress. Never reads game source directly.

agents.name: Mod Builder
agents.description: Implements the in-game mod — state reading, action execution, IPC server. Expert on the game engine's internals and modding framework.

agents.name: Bot Builder
agents.description: Implements the external bot — strategy engine, LLM integration, learning system, event bus for overlay.

agents.name: Protocol Engineer
agents.description: Owns the IPC layer between mod and bot. Ensures both sides speak the same protocol, tools are defined consistently.

agents.name: Analyst
agents.description: Game strategy expert and prompt engineer. Designs decision prompts, reviews run data, maintains the learning system.

agents.name: Play Operator
agents.description: Operates self-play sessions — launch, monitor, collect results, report metrics, trigger learning cycles.

agents.name: Overlay Developer
agents.description: Builds the OBS streaming overlay — WebSocket client, real-time display of reasoning, stats, and learning.

agents.name: Cycle Agent
agents.description: Disposable build-test-fix worker. One fix per spawn, reads checkpoint, returns short summary.

agents.name: Skeptic
agents.description: Reviews cross-component consistency — protocol schemas match, game knowledge is accurate, edge cases handled.

shared: Checkpoint — cycle count, current blocker, what works, what to never do
Section index — line ranges for navigating large source files
Strategy files — LLM-authored game strategy with confidence tiers
private: Run data — per-run journals with decisions, outcomes, death causes
memoryArchitecture.description: Checkpoint-driven coordination. Strategy files evolve through a confidence pipeline (hypothesis → low → medium → high) based on run evidence. Only medium+ confidence gets compiled into LLM prompts.

diagram:
graph TD
  O[Orchestrator] -->|spawn| MB[Mod Builder]
  O -->|spawn| BB[Bot Builder]
  O -->|spawn| ME[Protocol Engineer]
  O -->|spawn| AN[Analyst]
  O -->|spawn| PO[Play Operator]
  O -->|spawn| OD[Overlay Dev]
  O -->|spawn| CY[Cycle Agent]
  O -->|spawn| SK[Skeptic]
  subgraph Game Process
    MOD[Mod] -->|state/actions| IPC[IPC Protocol]
  end
  subgraph Bot Process
    BOT[Bot] -->|requests| IPC
    BOT -->|decisions| LLM[Claude API]
    BOT -->|events| WS[WebSocket]
  end
  subgraph OBS
    OV[Overlay] -->|connect| WS
  end

name: Steward Bootstrap
summary: A single steward agent handles all work for a small project, self-manages its context, and proposes role splits when complexity demands it.
description: Instead of designing a multi-agent system upfront, start with one steward who knows the conventions (role files, memory, growth path) and does everything. The steward manages its own context cleanup, maintains a single memory file, and monitors whether it's context-switching too heavily across unrelated domains. When it judges the time is right, it proposes a split — not because a template says to, but because the work has grown enough to justify it. The value is the bootstrap prompt that teaches ecosystem conventions, making the project self-sufficient from day one.
realWorldExample: rhizome (agent pattern catalog) runs with a single steward handling frontend, data curation, API, build pipeline, and deployment. After its first real session, the steward produced well-structured memory covering bug fixes, accessibility improvements, and data quality work across all domains. After months of use, it still hasn't proposed a role split — correctly, given the project's scope. ops (deployment infrastructure) also uses a steward, managing cross-project deployment queues and health monitoring.
whenToUse: New projects, small-scope projects, or cross-cutting service projects where upfront multi-agent design would be premature. The steward grows the agent system organically based on actual complexity, not predicted complexity.
strengths: Zero overhead — no protocol, no forum, no coordination cost
Organic growth — splits happen when needed, not when a template says so
Full context — one agent sees everything, no information loss across role boundaries
Self-sufficient — the bootstrap prompt teaches conventions so the project can evolve independently
Validated — multiple projects confirm the steward correctly delays splitting
weaknesses: Context limits — single agent eventually hits context window pressure with large projects
No specialization — jack of all trades, master of none (fine for small scope)
No adversarial review — no skeptic to challenge decisions (acceptable at this scale)
Growth judgment is subjective — steward may delay splitting too long or propose it too early

agents.name: Steward
agents.description: Sole agent handling all work. Self-manages context, maintains memory, monitors complexity, and proposes role splits when the work outgrows a single agent. Knows the full growth path: 2-3 agents → add forum, 3+ → add protocol, heavy docs → split into ref/, drifting docs → add librarian.

private: memory/steward.md — session notes, known issues, observations, remaining work
memoryArchitecture.description: Minimal. One memory file maintained by the steward. No shared state needed when there's only one agent. Memory structure grows if/when the steward splits into multiple roles.

diagram:
graph TD
  S[Steward] -->|all work| CODE[Codebase]
  S -->|maintains| MEM[Memory]
  S -->|monitors| COMPLEXITY{Complexity\nGrowing?}
  COMPLEXITY -->|no| S
  COMPLEXITY -->|yes| SPLIT[Propose Role Split]
  SPLIT -->|creates| A1[Agent 1]
  SPLIT -->|creates| A2[Agent 2]
  SPLIT -->|adds| F[Forum]

name: Agent Ecosystem Federation
summary: Autonomous projects with different internal agent structures, governed by a shared forge, serviced by shared ops, with patterns flowing through a catalog and template library.
description: Multiple projects coexist in an ecosystem, each choosing its own internal coordination model (protocol+forum, checkpoint cycles, or single steward) based on its needs. A forge meta-manages all agent systems — scanning for gaps, propagating upgrades, and maintaining consistency without imposing uniformity. An ops steward provides cross-project deployment as a shared service. A pattern catalog captures what actually works in practice, and a template library provides starting points for new projects. Patterns flow upward from real usage into the catalog, get extracted into templates, and seed new projects — a living knowledge system.
realWorldExample: The thisminute ecosystem runs 8 projects: thisminute (10 agents, protocol+forum), mainmenu (7 agents, protocol+forum), sts2 (9 agents, checkpoint), balatro (9 agents, checkpoint), rhizome (1 steward), ops (1 steward), forge.thisminute.org (3 agents, checkpoint), and agent-forge (4 template roles). The forge has run 13 audit cycles, during which it cleaned forums across projects, retired the messages/ pattern ecosystem-wide, bootstrapped steward agents, added librarian roles, and extracted 6 validated patterns into the catalog. Ops handles deployment queues for all projects. The template library seeded balatro's entire agent system from sts2's proven structure in one session.
whenToUse: When you have 3+ projects that benefit from shared agent system patterns but have different enough domains that one-size-fits-all coordination would be counterproductive. The federation lets each project evolve independently while shared governance prevents drift and captures cross-cutting lessons.
strengths: Autonomy with consistency — projects choose their own coordination model but share patterns and governance
Knowledge compounds — lessons from one project improve all others through the pattern pipeline
Shared services reduce duplication — one ops steward deploys everything, one forge audits everything
New projects bootstrap fast — template library provides a proven starting point, not a blank slate
Organic evolution — the catalog captures what emerged from practice, not what was designed upfront
weaknesses: Governance overhead — the forge, ops, catalog, and templates are themselves projects that need maintenance
Pattern extraction lag — real usage outpaces documentation; the catalog is always slightly behind practice
Cross-project changes are risky — a smith upgrading one project can conflict with active work
Federation requires a human steward — someone has to decide when to extract patterns, when to defer, and when a project's structure is 'good enough'

agents.name: Forge
agents.description: Meta-agent system (forgemaster, assayer, smith, keeper) that audits all projects' agent systems against the pattern library and applies upgrades. Operates on a scan-upgrade-maintain cycle.

agents.name: Ops Steward
agents.description: Cross-project deployment service. Manages deploy queues, runs tests, pushes code, deploys infrastructure, verifies health. Single agent serving all projects.

agents.name: Pattern Catalog
agents.description: Browsable taxonomy of validated orchestration patterns (rhizome). Includes field-tested patterns from the ecosystem alongside theoretical patterns. The forge contributes validated patterns; the catalog informs new project setup.

agents.name: Template Library
agents.description: Generic agent system templates (agent-forge). Provides starter patterns, role files, and coordination models for new projects. Updated when patterns mature through real usage.

agents.name: Product Team
agents.description: Individual project agent systems (4-10 agents each). Each team picks its own coordination model — protocol+forum for steady-state products, checkpoint for rapid iteration, steward for small scope.

shared: Audit file — cumulative cross-project gap analysis maintained by the forge
Pattern catalog — 200+ orchestration patterns including field-tested entries from the ecosystem
Template library — starter patterns and role files for bootstrapping new projects
Deploy queue — cross-project deployment requests handled by the ops steward
private: Per-project agent state — each project maintains its own forum/checkpoint/memory independently
Forge memory — lessons about managing agent systems across the portfolio
memoryArchitecture.description: Layered shared state. Each project owns its internal coordination state. The forge maintains cross-project audit state. The pattern catalog and template library are shared knowledge that flows between the forge and individual projects. The deploy queue is a cross-cutting service channel.

diagram:
graph TD
  subgraph Governance
    FORGE[Forge] -->|audit & upgrade| P1
    FORGE -->|audit & upgrade| P2
    FORGE -->|audit & upgrade| P3
    FORGE -->|extract patterns| CAT[Pattern Catalog]
    FORGE -->|update templates| TPL[Template Library]
  end
  subgraph Shared Services
    OPS[Ops Steward] -->|deploy| P1
    OPS -->|deploy| P2
    OPS -->|deploy| P3
  end
  subgraph Projects
    P1[Product A\n10 agents\nprotocol+forum]
    P2[Product B\n9 agents\ncheckpoint]
    P3[Product C\n1 steward]
  end
  TPL -->|seed new project| P4[New Project]
  CAT -->|inform setup| TPL
  P1 -.->|real usage| FORGE
  P2 -.->|real usage| FORGE
  P3 -.->|real usage| FORGE
